schema.title: Response Login V1 Auth Login Post
Response List Addresses V1 Addresses Get

post.description:
Chat/thread attachments. Deliberately separate from /image (admin-only,
strict image allowlist) and /artwork (checkout-only, unauthenticated,
hardcodes kind="file") -- this one needs to work for both customers and
admins, needs real image/video/file kind detection, and persists a row
per file (via UploadService) so a later POST /threads/:id/messages call
with `attachmentIds` can resolve those ids back to real, owned metadata
instead of trusting whatever the client sends.

Rate limited slightly looser than /artwork (15/min vs 5/min): this is
meant to be used repeatedly through the course of a conversation, not
once at checkout.

Rate limited: each call creates a DB row AND starts a live Paystack
transaction (a real API call, with a real cost/quota on Paystack's
side) -- this is the checkout endpoint, not something a legitimate
client should be hitting in a tight loop.

get.description:
Called by the frontend checkout confirmation screen (step 12) after
Paystack redirects back with `?reference=`. Doesn't just read back
whatever's in the DB -- if the order is still PENDING_PAYMENT, this
asks Paystack directly rather than assuming the webhook already landed.

Rate limited: this is a one-shot reconciliation call that hits Paystack
on our behalf, not the polling endpoint -- see /{id}/payment-status for
that. 10/minute is generous for "called once when the page loads" and
still caps how many Paystack verify calls a single client can trigger.

The polling endpoint the confirmation page hits on a timer while an
order is PENDING_PAYMENT. Pure local DB read -- no Paystack call, so
it's cheap enough to poll every few seconds. That's also exactly why it
needs its own (higher) rate limit: unlike the rest of this router, this
one is *expected* to be called repeatedly by the same client in a short
window, so the limit exists to catch a runaway/misbehaving poll loop
(e.g. an interval that never got cleared) rather than normal usage.

post.description:
Rate limited: internally calls create_new_order, so it carries the
same cost (DB write + live Paystack transaction) as POST /orders.

Rate limited (30/min): a real DB write plus a websocket broadcast to
everyone connected to the thread. Higher than the order-mutation
endpoints (20/min) since this is meant to be used repeatedly through an
ongoing conversation, but still capped against a scripted flood.

Returns the full thread (not just the new message) so the client sees
its own message land -- with server-assigned id/timestamp/resolved
attachments -- without a second round trip.

No auth -- this is the public order-lookup form. Rate limited harder
than most GETs in this app on purpose: it's an unauthenticated
id+email lookup, i.e. exactly the shape of an enumeration/brute-force
target (guess order ids against a known email, or vice versa). 10/min
per client is enough for a real customer checking their own order a
few times, not enough for a scripted sweep.

No auth -- a public lead-gen form, same category as /track and the
Paystack webhook in terms of being reachable by anyone. Rate limited
tighter than most public endpoints (5/min) since this writes a DB row
and fires a notification task on every call with no ownership check
at all -- the cheapest form on the site to spam.

AttachmentResponse.title: AttachmentResponse
AttachmentResponse.description:
Response shape for POST /uploads/attachments -- used for chat
message attachments. `kind` is a real Literal (not a plain str like
ArtworkFileResponse's) since it's derived server-side from the actual
content-type rather than hardcoded, and downstream (thread messages)
depends on it being exactly image|video|file.

AttachmentSchema.title: AttachmentSchema
AttachmentSchema.description:
Same shape as uploads.schemas.AttachmentResponse -- duplicated here
rather than imported so the threads app doesn't take on a hard schema
dependency on uploads; the two are allowed to drift independently if
either side's response shape needs to change later.

CategoryUpdate.title: CategoryUpdate
CategoryUpdate.description:
PATCH payload for a category. Every field is optional; the service
layer only touches fields the caller actually sent
(`model_dump(exclude_unset=True)`), so omitted fields are left alone
rather than reset to `None`.

message.title: Message
message.default: Design request submitted. Our team will reach out within 24 hours.

LoginResponse.title: LoginResponse
LoginResponse.description:
The token pair sent back to the client, omitting the refresh token 
since it lives in an HttpOnly cookie.

OptionGroupCreate.title: OptionGroupCreate
OptionGroupCreate.description:
Write-side option group, used both on product create and on the
`options` field of a product update (a full-replace, not a merge).

OptionGroupResponse.title: OptionGroupResponse
OptionGroupResponse.description:
Read-side view of a `ProductOptionGroup`. The `id` is derived from
`label` via `slugify` rather than read off the ORM row, since the ORM's
own `id` is just an opaque DB uuid that the frontend never sees.

OptionValue.title: OptionValue
OptionValue.description:
A single selectable value within an option group, e.g. {"id": "small",
"label": "Small", "priceMultiplier": 1.0}.

OptionValueCreate.title: OptionValueCreate
OptionValueCreate.description:
Write-side option value. Unlike `OptionValue`, `id` is caller-supplied
(it's the key used in POST /pricing/quote's `options` dict), so it must
stay unique within its parent group — enforced by
`OptionGroupCreate.unique_value_ids`.

isFinal.title: Isfinal
isFinal.description: True once status is no longer 'Pending Payment' -- tells the client to stop polling.

PaymentStatusResponse.title: PaymentStatusResponse
PaymentStatusResponse.description:
Deliberately minimal -- the frontend polls GET /orders/{id}/payment-status
on the confirmation page and just needs to know whether to keep waiting.
This is NOT the reconciliation call (that's still /orders/verify-payment,
meant to be hit once since it talks to Paystack) -- this one only ever
reads local DB state, which is what makes it safe to poll frequently.

ProductUpdate.title: ProductUpdate
ProductUpdate.description:
PATCH payload for a product. Every field is optional and only
fields explicitly sent by the caller are applied
(`model_dump(exclude_unset=True)`).

`options` and `quantityTiers`, when sent, are treated as a full
replacement of the product's existing groups/tiers rather than a merge —
matching how they're supplied wholesale on create.

QuoteRequest.title: QuoteRequest
QuoteRequest.description:
Body for POST /pricing/quote. `options` maps an option-group's
slugified label (e.g. "print-finish") to the chosen value's `id`.

RefreshResponse.title: RefreshResponse
RefreshResponse.description:
The refresh token itself is no longer echoed here — it lives only in
the HttpOnly cookie the router sets/reads directly.

SignupRequest.title: SignupRequest
SignupRequest.description:
Matches the frontend signup form: fullname, email, phone, password +
confirm password. No company, no role.

ThreadSchema.title: ThreadSchema
ThreadSchema.description:
Full thread -- backs GET /threads/:id, POST /threads, and the
response of POST /threads/:id/messages (returns the whole thread so the
client doesn't need a second round trip to see its own message land).

ThreadSummarySchema.title: ThreadSummarySchema
ThreadSummarySchema.description:
Backs GET /threads (the inbox list view). Carries only the last
message as a preview instead of full history -- the list view doesn't
need it, and loading every message for every thread on every page of
the inbox would be needlessly expensive.
